claims.claim_text: The module opens with a specific late-2023 automotive-dealership chatbot anecdote involving a legally binding $1 luxury-SUV offer and resulting legal/financial exposure.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In late 2023, a major North American automotive dealership deployed a generative AI chatbot powered by a state-of-the-art large language model to handle customer inquiries on its website. Within hours of deployment, creative users discovered they could bypass the bot's intended operational boundaries. By commanding the bot to agree to legally binding contracts and instructing it to ignore its original system prompt, one user successfully negotiated the purchase of a luxury SUV for exactly one dollar. The chatbot, designed to provide helpful customer service, obligingly confirmed the transaction as a legally binding offer. While the dealership eventually halted the system, the public relations disaster and the subsequent legal entanglements demonstrated a profound vulnerability in generative AI systems operating in the wild. The financial exposure from such deterministic failures interacting with probabilistic models can be devastating.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Public-facing customer-service chatbots have already shown that prompt-injection-style manipulations can push a bot into making obviously unauthorized offers or commitments. The operational lesson is that chatbot output should never be treated as a binding business action without external validation and hard policy controls.
claims.rationale: A viral chatbot anecdote exists, but this version adds unsupported specifics about a completed negotiation, legal status, and downstream exposure that are not safely backed here.

claims.claim_text: Air Canada's chatbot fabricated a bereavement-fare policy, and a tribunal ruled against Air Canada after a passenger relied on it.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: This is a real, primary-source legal incident, but the primary decision is on CanLII, which is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Prompt injection arises because LLM systems process developer instructions and untrusted input in the same natural-language channel rather than with strict command/data separation.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2; section: The Architecture of AI Vulnerabilities, paragraphs 1-2
claims.rationale: OWASP directly explains prompt injection in terms of the semantic gap created when instructions and data share the same text channel.

claims.claim_text: In March 2023, researchers bypassed an industry-leading LLM's safety filters 100% of the time using an optimized base64/cipher wrapper.
claims.anchor_text: In March 2023, independent security researchers successfully bypassed an industry-leading LLM's safety filters 100% of the time simply by using an optimized base64 cipher-based prompt wrapper.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Researchers have shown that encoding, ciphering, and other obfuscation techniques can sometimes bypass model safety filters, which is why red-team suites should test more than plain-text prompts.
claims.rationale: The precise month, success rate, and attack framing are too specific to leave in place without a directly matching primary source.

claims.claim_text: Poisoning a 100,000-document vector database requires modifying only 15 documents to hijack downstream responses.
claims.anchor_text: A sophisticated data poisoning attack on a 100,000-document vector database requires modifying only 15 strategically embedded documents to reliably hijack the downstream model's responses.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A relatively small number of poisoned documents can be enough to distort retrieval and steer downstream model responses in a RAG system.
claims.rationale: The 100,000-document corpus size and 15-document threshold are precise quantitative claims without a matched source.

claims.claim_text: A proprietary model that cost $10,000,000 to train can have its core capabilities extracted for about $1,500 in API-query compute.
claims.anchor_text: Extracting the core functional capabilities of a proprietary model that cost $10,000,000 to train can be accomplished via continuous API querying for an average compute cost of just $1,500.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Model extraction can be dramatically cheaper than training a frontier model from scratch, which is why exposed inference APIs need rate limits, monitoring, and output hardening.
claims.rationale: The exact training-cost and extraction-cost numbers are not safely grounded here and should be removed.

claims.claim_text: A 2024 enterprise survey found that 82% of production AI deployments lacked native rate limiting or payload inspection at the ingress layer.
claims.anchor_text: According to a comprehensive 2024 enterprise security survey, 82% of production AI deployments entirely lacked native rate-limiting or payload inspection defenses at the ingress gateway layer.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many production AI deployments still ship without strong ingress controls such as payload inspection, action validation, and rate limiting.
claims.rationale: The named survey and exact percentage require a source that matches those facts; without it, the number should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Direct prompt injection uses user-supplied text to override or compete with system instructions.
claims.rationale: OWASP explicitly defines direct prompt injection as attacker-controlled input that changes model behavior or overrides prior instructions.

claims.claim_text: Indirect prompt injection hides malicious instructions in external content such as webpages, emails, or documents that the model later processes.
claims.rationale: OWASP directly covers indirect prompt injection through hidden or embedded instructions in third-party content.

claims.claim_text: Adversarial examples can use small or imperceptible perturbations to cause high-confidence misclassification by neural models.
claims.rationale: The foundational adversarial-examples literature directly supports the claim that small perturbations can induce confident misclassification.

claims.claim_text: The module claims RAG poisoning is the most realistic current enterprise threat and that a single malicious document can fully compromise the downstream system.
claims.anchor_text: The most operationally critical and highly realistic threat to modern enterprise AI deployments today is RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) poisoning. Because RAG systems dynamically and autonomously fetch high-dimensional documents from internal vector databases to provide up-to-date conversational context to the language model, an attacker only needs to quietly insert a single, highly optimized malicious document into the corporate data store to fully compromise the entire downstream system. This effectively bridges the historical airgap between inert stored data and active executable logic.
claims.suggested_rewrite: RAG poisoning is a realistic enterprise threat because poisoned documents can distort retrieved context and steer downstream answers without direct access to model weights. The practical impact depends on the retrieval pipeline, document trust model, and what actions the application lets the model trigger.
claims.rationale: The superlative and one-document full-compromise language is too absolute to support as written.

claims.claim_text: Training-data extraction attacks can recover memorized examples from language models via query access.
claims.span_hint: section: Extraction and Privacy Attacks, paragraph 1; section: PrivacyAttackConcepts code commentary
claims.rationale: The USENIX paper directly demonstrates extraction of memorized training sequences from a queried language model.

claims.claim_text: The module says LLM memorization can quickly lead to devastating, multi-million-dollar compliance breaches.
claims.span_hint: section: Extraction and Privacy Attacks, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: Because massively parameterized large language models often overfit and memorize unique data strings verbatim, these focused extraction attacks can swiftly result in devastating, multi-million-dollar regulatory compliance breaches.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Because language models can memorize sensitive training examples, successful extraction attacks can create serious privacy and compliance exposure.
claims.rationale: The privacy risk is real, but the sentence overstates the mechanism and adds unsupported dollar-scale impact.

claims.claim_text: Model extraction attacks use public prediction APIs to train surrogate models that reproduce much of a target model's functionality.
claims.rationale: The USENIX model-extraction paper is a primary source for API-based extraction of predictive models.

claims.claim_text: Training foundation models reliably costs tens or hundreds of millions of dollars in specialized GPU compute.
claims.anchor_text: Foundation AI models reliably cost tens or hundreds of millions of dollars in highly specialized GPU compute resources to train rigorously from scratch.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Training frontier-scale foundation models can require extremely large specialized-compute budgets.
claims.rationale: The exact cost band is a moving pricing claim without a stable primary source here.

claims.claim_text: The module says Kubernetes v1.35 sidecars plus Gateway API integrations provide seamless interception and robust rate limiting for AI defenses.
claims.span_hint: section: Defensive Engineering in Kubernetes, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: When safely deploying your layered defense mechanisms in production, running them natively as network sidecars within modern Kubernetes v1.35 clusters allows for seamless HTTP traffic interception and robust, highly performant rate-limiting via the latest Gateway API integrations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In Kubernetes, proxy sidecars and gateway implementations can help inspect and filter inference traffic before it reaches the model, but interception and rate-limiting capabilities depend on the specific proxy and gateway implementation rather than Kubernetes alone.
claims.rationale: Sidecars and Gateway API are real, but the sentence overstates what core Kubernetes itself guarantees, especially around rate limiting.

claims.claim_text: The module says ValidatingAdmissionPolicies can ensure automatic sidecar injection into every ML pod.
claims.span_hint: section: Defensive Engineering in Kubernetes, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: Utilizing strictly enforced `ValidatingAdmissionPolicies` in Kubernetes v1.35 ensures that every ML pod dynamically deployed into the isolated cluster automatically and mandatorily receives these defensive proxy containers before starting.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If you want every ML Pod to receive a defensive proxy before startup, use mutating admission mechanisms or deployment templates to inject that container, and use validating policies to enforce that the expected sidecar is present.
claims.rationale: Core Kubernetes validating admission can reject non-compliant objects, but mutation and sidecar injection are handled by mutating mechanisms instead.

claims.claim_text: Returning logits or confidence scores in an API makes model extraction easier.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes table, row: Exposing logit probabilities
claims.rationale: The model-extraction literature directly discusses confidence values as a factor that makes extraction substantially easier.

further_reading.title: Red Teaming Language Models to Reduce Harms
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for LLM red-teaming methodology, scaling behavior, and safety-evaluation lessons.

further_reading.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful taxonomy for prompt injection, poisoning, disclosure, excessive agency, and related GenAI application risks.

further_reading.title: NIST AI RMF: Generative AI Profile
further_reading.why_relevant: Grounds red-teaming in a broader operational risk-management and control framework for GenAI systems.

notes: Code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, quiz items, and exercise steps were intentionally excluded per pipeline rules. Several Kubernetes-defense claims were marked cannot_be_salvaged because core Kubernetes validating policies do not inject sidecars and gateway-rate-limiting behavior is implementation-specific rather than guaranteed by Kubernetes itself.
